verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is canonically a nuclear chromatin protein but has documented outer-leaflet display in specific cell states. Platelets were among the first cells shown to display HMGB1 on their surface (originally named amphoterin), and HMGB1 is detected on platelet- and monocyte-derived microparticles by flow cytometry after LPS activation. In developing neurons, membrane-associated HMGB1/amphoterin localizes to growth cones. Activated monocytes/macrophages translocate HMGB1 to secretory lysosomes that fuse with the PM, and necroptotic/pyroptotic cells externalize it. It also colocalizes with αvβ3 on macrophage surfaces via PS binding. These are cell-state-induced (activation, cell death, platelet display) surface pools — not its own TM/GPI anchor — so contextual rather than yes. Anti-HMGB1 clinical/preclinical programs target the released soluble form.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Most surface 'display' is non-covalent association with PS/integrins on activated/dying cells; borderline between cell_state_induced and secreted_only recruitment.
